title: Quiz: Quantum Feature Encoding
description: Test your understanding of techniques for encoding credit data into quantum states

questions.question: How many qubits does amplitude encoding require for N features?
questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding maps N features to log₂(N) qubits by storing the feature values as amplitudes of a quantum state. With n qubits, you can encode 2^n features, so you need only log₂(N) qubits for N features.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of angle encoding?

options.text: Uses fewer qubits than other methods

questions.explanation: Angle encoding uses only single-qubit rotation gates (like R_y), making it very shallow and suitable for NISQ devices. However, it requires one qubit per feature, which limits scalability.

questions.question: Which encoding method is best for categorical features like 'employment status'?
questions.explanation: Basis encoding is ideal for categorical features because it maps discrete values directly to computational basis states. For example, 4 employment categories need only 2 qubits: |00⟩, |01⟩, |10⟩, |11⟩.

questions.question: What is the 'data loading bottleneck' in amplitude encoding?

options.text: The encoded state is too small
Preparing arbitrary amplitude states may require O(N) or more gates
Only positive values can be encoded

questions.explanation: While amplitude encoding uses only log(N) qubits, preparing an arbitrary state with N amplitudes typically requires O(N) gates, potentially eliminating the computational advantage. Efficient loading is an active research area.

questions.question: In IQP-style encoding, all basis state amplitudes have the same magnitude, with information stored in the phases.
questions.explanation: True. IQP encoding starts with a uniform superposition (equal amplitudes) and applies diagonal phase gates. The feature information is encoded in the relative phases between basis states, not the amplitudes.

questions.question: For a credit application with 1000 features, which encoding uses fewer qubits?
questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding needs log₂(1000) ≈ 10 qubits to encode 1000 features (since 2^10 = 1024). Angle encoding would need 1000 qubits (one per feature). This qubit efficiency is amplitude encoding's main advantage.

questions.question: Why does IQP encoding include ZZ interaction terms?

options.text: To reduce the number of qubits needed
To capture feature interactions (like x_i × x_j)

questions.explanation: ZZ interaction terms in IQP encoding create phases that depend on products of features (x_i × x_j), allowing the kernel to capture non-linear relationships between features that single-feature encodings miss.

questions.question: Which are valid considerations when choosing an encoding for credit data? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Circuit depth limits of the hardware
Whether features are categorical or continuous

questions.explanation: Valid considerations include: (a) qubit count determines whether angle or amplitude encoding is feasible, (b) circuit depth limits affect amplitude encoding viability, (c) data types determine whether basis or angle encoding is appropriate.
